lines: the analytics database and return rows as JSON.',

followups.q: How does tool_choice affect which tools the Claude API will call?
followups.a: tool_choice has three modes: 'auto' (model decides whether to call a tool, which tool, and when to stop), 'any' (model must call at least one tool this turn), and {'type': 'tool', 'name': 'X'} (forces the model to call tool X this turn). Use 'any' or forced tool_choice when you need guaranteed structured output from a specific tool and the task is unambiguous — it eliminates the model opting to respond in plain text when you expected a structured call.

q: What is the Claude API's context window and how do you handle long conversations that approach the limit without losing coherence?
a: Claude 3.5 and claude-opus-4-5 / claude-sonnet-4-5 support 200,000 input tokens as of early 2026. You hit the limit before you expect because the full conversation history is sent on every turn; a 1,000-turn chat at 100 tokens per turn is already 100,000 input tokens. The standard patterns for management are: summarize-and-compress (periodically call the Claude API to compress the middle of the conversation into a dense summary, then replace those turns with the summary); sliding-window (keep only the system prompt, the last N turns, and the summary); and retrieval-augmented history (embed conversation turns and retrieve the most relevant ones rather than the full history). Track input_tokens from the Claude API response usage on every call and trigger compression when you cross 60-70% of the context window.
fj: If the context fills up I just start a new conversation.
fs: Track input_tokens from the Claude API response on every call; trigger summarize-and-compress at 60-70% of the 200K window — new conversation loses continuity, compression preserves it.

followups.q: How do you write a compress_history call to the Claude API that produces a useful summary?
followups.a: Prompt the Claude API to produce a dense first-person narrative summary: 'Summarize the following conversation turns into a concise paragraph preserving key decisions, facts established, and any user preferences. Use past tense.' Include the turns as a single user message. Insert the result as an assistant turn with a prefix like '[Prior conversation summarized]'. Keep the summary under 500 tokens to ensure compression actually reduces context size.

q: How do you add observability to Claude API calls in production — what should you log, trace, and alert on?
a: Every Claude API call should emit: a structured log entry with model, input_tokens, output_tokens, cache_read_input_tokens, cache_creation_input_tokens, stop_reason, latency_ms, and your own metadata (feature_name, user_id, request_id). Trace the full agent loop if applicable — one distributed trace spanning multiple API calls per user request. Alert on: error_rate > 1% (5xx or 400 from the Claude API), p95 latency > 5s, anomalous spend spikes (possible key leak), and stop_reason = 'max_tokens' rate > 2% (output being truncated). PII scrub before logging — never write raw user messages to logs without redaction. Store logs in a way that lets you replay any production call in your eval harness to debug a regression.
fj: I just log the model's response text so I can see what it said.
fs: Log tokens/cost/stop_reason/latency per call, trace multi-turn agent loops, alert on error rate + latency + spend spikes + truncation rate — and PII-scrub before writing.

followups.q: How does routing Claude API calls through an LLM gateway improve observability versus direct SDK calls?
followups.a: An LLM gateway centralizes all Claude API call logs, costs, and errors in one place regardless of which microservice made the call. Each service only needs to send its metadata tag; the gateway records the token counts, provider response codes, and latency. You get a unified cost dashboard, cross-service error rate, and a single place to set alerts — instead of aggregating logs from N services separately.

q: How do you implement retry and error handling for the Claude API in production, covering both transient and hard failures?
a: The Anthropic SDK retries automatically with exponential backoff on 429 (rate limit) and 5xx errors, defaulting to 2 retries. In production, set max_retries=3 and add a timeout parameter (connect_timeout + read_timeout) so a hanging connection does not block a thread indefinitely. Classify errors: 429 is transient (rate limit, always retry with backoff), 500/529 are transient (provider overloaded), 400 is client error (bad schema, context overflow — do not retry, fix the request), 401 is credentials failure (don't retry, alert). Add a circuit breaker that opens after N consecutive 5xx responses and routes to a fallback model via an LLM gateway. Log the error type, model, and input token count on every failure so you can correlate errors with specific call patterns.
fj: If the API call fails I catch the exception and tell the user to try again.
fs: 429/5xx are transient → SDK auto-retries with backoff; 400 is client error → fix the call; 401 → alert immediately; add circuit breaker via LLM gateway for sustained outages.

followups.q: What is error code 529 from the Claude API and how does it differ from 500?
followups.a: 529 is Anthropic's 'API overloaded' response, meaning their servers are at capacity and the request was shed — a transient capacity issue, not an application error. Unlike a 500 which signals an internal server error on a specific request, a 529 means Anthropic is healthy but busy. Both are retryable, but 529 specifically warrants routing to an LLM gateway fallback model if it persists, since it signals sustained provider load rather than a one-off error.

followups.q: How do you implement a circuit breaker on Claude API calls without a gateway?
followups.a: Track consecutive failure counts in a shared counter (Redis or in-process for single instances). After N consecutive 5xx responses within a time window, flip a flag to 'open' and route to a fallback (cached response, degraded mode, or alternative provider). After a cooldown period, flip to 'half-open' and let one probe request through. If it succeeds, close the circuit. If not, stay open. The same pattern is built into LiteLLM gateway's cooldown_time setting, which is why using an LLM gateway removes this from every individual service.

q: How do you use the Claude API's vision capability for document understanding pipelines — what are the input formats, token costs, and production trade-offs?
a: The Claude API accepts images as base64-encoded blocks or URL references in the messages content list, supporting JPEG, PNG, GIF, and WebP up to 5 MB per image and up to 20 images per request. Token cost for images is significant: a 1568×1568 image costs approximately 1,600 input tokens (roughly $0.0048 at Haiku rates), compared to ~400 tokens for the same content described in text — images are 4x more expensive per equivalent information. In document pipelines, the decision is: use vision for layouts where text extraction destroys structure (tables, charts, forms), use text extraction for dense prose where a PDF parser gives clean text cheaper and faster. Batch vision requests via the Message Batches API for offline document processing to get the 50% discount. Never resize below the model's minimum 1:1 pixel ratio or the model hallucinates image content.
fj: I just send the image URL and the Claude API understands it.
fs: Claude API vision: base64 or URL, max 5 MB, ~1,600 tokens/full-res image — use for layout-dependent content (tables/forms), text extraction for prose; batch via Message Batches for 50% off.

followups.q: When does sending a resized image to the Claude API give worse results than the original?
followups.a: Claude's vision scales images internally and is optimized for common aspect ratios. Resizing below ~768px on the short side degrades text readability in the image, causing OCR-style hallucinations on small fonts or fine grid lines. Also, extreme non-standard aspect ratios (very wide or tall images) may cause the model to miss content at the edges. For document pages, send at 1024-1568px width and let the API handle scaling.

q: How do you model LLM gateway latency overhead in a system design, and what SLAs should gateway-layer components target?
a: An LLM gateway sits in the critical path of every LLM call, so its added latency directly increases user-visible TTFB. Target < 5ms p50 and < 15ms p99 added latency — benchmarked under production load, not synthetic. The latency budget breaks down as: TLS handshake (amortized via connection pooling to near zero), routing table lookup (< 1ms in-memory), optional semantic cache check (2-5ms for a Redis hit), and response logging (async, non-blocking). Synchronous PII scanning at the gateway adds 5-20ms and must be weighed against security benefit; async PII logging (scan a copy, don't block the path) is the common compromise. Self-hosted LiteLLM proxy adds ~3ms p50 in practice on warm connections. If p99 overhead exceeds 30ms, re-architecture the gateway to move heavy middleware (guardrails, eval) off the hot path.
fj: The gateway is just a proxy so it adds barely any latency.
fs: LLM gateway budget: < 5ms p50 / < 15ms p99 added overhead; connection pool amortizes TLS; async log; move synchronous guardrails off hot path if p99 > 30ms.

followups.q: How do you benchmark an LLM gateway's overhead without polluting results with model generation time?
followups.a: Use a stub provider backend that returns an instant fixed response — a mock server or a single-token max_tokens=1 call. Measure round-trip time through the gateway versus direct to the stub. The difference is gateway overhead. Run with a realistic concurrent request load (50-200 RPS) to expose mutex contention, connection pool exhaustion, and per-request memory allocations that only appear under load.

q: What is semantic caching at the LLM gateway level, when does it save meaningfully, and what are the cache invalidation pitfalls?
a: Semantic caching at the LLM gateway embeds each incoming request prompt and performs a nearest-neighbor lookup against cached prompt embeddings; if a similar enough prior request (cosine similarity above a threshold, typically 0.95-0.98) exists, the gateway returns the cached response without hitting the provider. This is different from Anthropic prompt caching — it skips the model call entirely. It saves meaningfully for high-volume, narrow-domain Q&A where many users ask semantically equivalent questions (support bots, FAQ pipelines). Pitfalls: (1) threshold calibration — too low and factually distinct questions share a cached answer; (2) stale responses — cached answers go invalid when underlying data changes, requiring TTL or event-driven invalidation; (3) non-determinism — the cache obscures the model's natural response variance, which matters for evals. Disable semantic caching for personalized, context-dependent, or time-sensitive prompts.
fj: Semantic caching is like a search index for AI responses — always use it.
fs: Semantic cache skips the model call on near-duplicate prompts; set threshold ≥ 0.95, add TTL, and disable for personalized/context-dependent prompts — stale cached answers cause silent correctness failures.

code.label: semantic cache in LiteLLM proxy config
lines: # via cache_control: no-cache in request metadata

followups.q: How do you measure semantic cache hit rate and set the right threshold in production?
followups.a: Log every cache decision (hit, miss, rejected due to threshold) with the similarity score. Plot the distribution of similarity scores for misses — if most cluster at 0.92-0.96 and you have high miss rate, consider lowering the threshold, but run a correctness eval first: sample 100 cached-response pairs and verify the cached answer is correct for the incoming query. The threshold is a quality gate — lower it only after validating correctness.

q: How do you evaluate Claude API output quality across model versions — what makes a good LLM-as-judge prompt and what calibration does it need?
a: An LLM-as-judge prompt asks a capable model (claude-opus-4-5 or equivalent) to score a response against criteria on a consistent scale. A well-structured judge prompt: (1) states the evaluation criteria explicitly (accuracy, faithfulness to context, format compliance, safety); (2) provides the full context the model had access to (don't let the judge see anything the generation model didn't); (3) uses a structured output score (1-5 or binary) with a chain-of-thought rationale before the score to improve reliability; (4) includes one or two calibration examples of a clearly good and clearly bad response to anchor the scale. Before using the judge in CI, calibrate it against 100 human-rated examples — it must exceed 85% inter-rater agreement with human evaluators to be trustworthy. Use the same judge model version across runs and pin it, otherwise the judge's behavior changes between Claude API version updates.
fj: I just ask GPT-4 if the answer looks good.
fs: LLM-as-judge needs explicit criteria, context isolation, chain-of-thought before score, calibration examples, and 85%+ human agreement before you trust it in CI — and pin the judge Claude API version.

code.label: Claude API judge prompt
lines: JUDGE = '''You are evaluating an AI assistant's response.
Criteria: (1) factual accuracy vs context, (2) format adherence.

followups.q: Why do you ask the judge to reason before giving a score?
followups.a: Chain-of-thought before the verdict — 'think first, then score' — dramatically reduces position bias and anchor effects in LLM-as-judge evaluations. Without reasoning first, the model anchors on surface features (response length, confident tone) rather than actual correctness. The written rationale also makes disagreements auditable: you can inspect why the judge scored low and fix the prompt or criteria rather than treating the score as a black box.

q: How do you implement request deduplication and idempotency for Claude API calls in a distributed system?
a: The Claude API does not natively provide idempotency keys as of early 2026 — each call is a new request. You implement idempotency in your application layer: hash the deterministic inputs (model, system prompt, messages, sampling params) into a content-addressable key, check a fast cache (Redis) before calling the Claude API, and store the response keyed to that hash with a TTL matching your freshness requirement. For non-deterministic tasks where the same input can produce different valid responses, skip idempotency caching. In distributed task queues, use exactly-once delivery semantics or a database lock on the input hash before dispatching the Claude API call, so duplicate tasks from at-least-once queues don't fire redundant API calls. Track dedup hit rate in your LLM gateway or application metrics — a high hit rate means you have upstream retry storm or duplicate submission logic to fix.
fj: I just let duplicate requests go through — the model handles it.
fs: Hash inputs to a content-addressable key, check Redis before calling the Claude API, store responses with TTL — and use a database lock in distributed queues to prevent duplicate dispatch.

followups.q: When should you NOT cache Claude API responses even if the inputs are identical?
followups.a: Skip caching for: calls where the user expects fresh results (news summaries, current-data queries), calls where temperature > 0 and the output variance is the feature (creative generation), calls with tool use where the tool results are real-time data (stock price, live DB query), and calls that include the current time in context. Cache only deterministic, time-insensitive generation — classification, extraction, and format-locked transformation tasks.

q: What is the LLM gateway pattern for A/B testing model versions, and how do you safely roll out a Claude API upgrade without regressions reaching all users?
a: An LLM gateway adds a routing layer that lets you split traffic by percentage between model versions — e.g. 10% to claude-opus-4-5 and 90% to the prior version — without any application code change. This is model canary deployment. Configure the router with weighted model_list entries and log which model served each response with a variant tag. Run your eval harness continuously on sampled live outputs per variant, comparing quality scores, error rates, and cost. Only promote the new version when quality is statistically non-inferior on real traffic, not just on your offline eval set. The LLM gateway must tag responses with the model_version so downstream logging, cost attribution, and anomaly detection know which variant served. A/B at the gateway level keeps the split opaque to application code and reversible in seconds by adjusting weights.
fj: I upgrade the model version in the code and redeploy — if something breaks I'll revert.
fs: Route 10% traffic to the new Claude API model version via LLM gateway weights, measure quality+errors on sampled live outputs, promote only on statistical non-inferiority — reversible in seconds without redeployment.

code.label: LLM gateway weighted A/B routing
lines: # both keys from env; gateway tags model_version

followups.q: What metric do you use to decide the canary Claude API version is safe to promote to 100%?
followups.a: Primary: quality score from your LLM-as-judge eval on sampled canary outputs vs control, with a minimum sample size (200+ for 5% sensitivity). Secondary: error rate, max_tokens truncation rate, and cost per 1K tokens. Treat it as a hypothesis test — canary must be statistically non-inferior at p < 0.05 on the quality metric and show no regression in error rate. Only then promote. If quality is ambiguous, hold at 10% longer rather than rushing the rollout.

q: How do you design an org-wide LLM platform — gateway, eval pipeline, model registry, and cost governance — so that 15+ teams can ship reliably without stepping on each other?
a: An LLM platform has four load-bearing layers. First, a central gateway (LiteLLM or Portkey) that holds all provider keys, enforces per-team virtual-key spend caps and TPM limits, routes to provider-agnostic model aliases, and emits structured call logs — teams call the alias, never a raw provider URL. Second, a model registry that maps aliases like 'llm/fast' and 'llm/smart' to current pinned model versions; a platform team cuts a release by updating the registry, not by touching 15 service repos. Third, a shared eval harness in CI: teams register task-specific golden sets; the platform runs them on every registry update and blocks the alias promotion if any team's quality SLO regresses. Fourth, cost governance: Grafana or a data warehouse dashboard showing spend per team-id and feature-tag extracted from gateway logs, with automated budget-exhaustion alerts. The principle is clear ownership boundaries — teams own their prompts and golden sets; the platform team owns the alias, gateway, and infra; neither blocks the other's deploys.
fj: We just give each team an API key and let them figure it out.
fs: Provider-agnostic aliases in a model registry + gateway enforcing spend caps + shared eval CI that blocks alias promotion on quality regression — teams own prompts, platform owns infra.

followups.q: How do you handle a team whose golden set is too narrow to catch cross-cutting regressions?
followups.a: Run a platform-owned shadow eval set that covers representative tasks across all major use-case categories — extraction, summarization, reasoning, code. This is separate from team golden sets and is the platform's safety net for alias promotions. Narrow team evals catch regressions specific to their feature; the shadow set catches broad capability degradation invisible to any single team's slice.

followups.q: How do you enforce model aliases without breaking teams that need to pin to a specific model version?
followups.a: Expose an escape hatch: teams can request a hard-pinned alias (e.g. 'llm/claude-opus-4-5-pinned') in the registry with an SLA that it will not be swapped. These require explicit opt-in and an owner who commits to migrating away before the provider deprecation date. Track hard-pinned aliases as tech debt in the platform backlog — they accumulate quickly if you don't gate them.

q: Design a build-vs-buy decision framework for LLM capabilities — when do you self-host open models, when do you use cloud APIs, and how do you quantify the crossover for a 50M-token/day workload?
a: Start with a four-axis decision matrix: data sensitivity (PII, HIPAA, regulated data — often forces self-host regardless of cost); capability gap (does any open model clear the quality SLO? If not, frontier API wins by default); volume/cost crossover (see below); and ops capacity (do you have a team to run a GPU cluster with vLLM, on-call, and model upgrades?). At 50M tokens/day (~1.5B/month) of mixed input/output, compare: frontier API (Claude Haiku at $0.80/1M = ~$1,200/month) versus self-hosted Llama-3.1-8B on 2× A100 80GB spot instances on GCP (~$3/h × 2 × 730h = $4,380/month raw, ~$1.5-2k amortized with high utilization and spot discounts) plus ~0.3 FTE SRE. At this volume the frontier API is often cheaper unless you use a model larger than 8B. The crossover for a well-utilized 70B model typically falls between 500M-2B tokens/month. The hybrid answer for most platforms: API for frontier tasks and bursty traffic, self-hosted fine-tuned model for high-volume, steady-state, latency-tolerant pipelines.
fj: Self-hosting is always cheaper at scale — just run Llama.
fs: Build the matrix: data sensitivity → PII forces self-host; capability gap → open model must clear the SLO; volume crossover for 70B typically 500M-2B tokens/month; ops cost is non-zero — hybrid usually wins.

code.label: cost crossover estimate
lines: # Break even needs high utilization + spot
# and open model clearing quality SLO

followups.q: How do you handle the capability gap if no open model clears your SLO today?
followups.a: Track the open-model quality curve — it closes fast. Run your eval set quarterly against current open releases (Llama, Qwen, Mistral) and update the decision memo. In the meantime, use the frontier API for the tasks that require it and route lower-complexity sub-tasks to open models to reduce API spend. Don't do a one-time analysis; the crossover date is predictable and you want to be ready when an open model clears the bar.

followups.q: What hidden costs make self-hosting more expensive than the GPU bill suggests?
followups.a: Model upgrade cadence (you re-evaluate every Llama or Qwen release, run benchmarks, re-quantize, and re-deploy), on-call and incident response for a high-availability serving cluster, CUDA and driver compatibility work on each upgrade, and the opportunity cost of the SRE time. A realistic SRE burden is 0.25-0.5 FTE for a mature 2-4 GPU cluster. Factor that into the crossover calculation at your team's labor cost.

q: How do you architect an LLM eval pipeline that serves as the release gate across multiple products, model versions, and business metrics simultaneously?
a: A production eval pipeline is a multi-stage gate, not a single test suite. Stage 1 runs in CI on every prompt or model alias change: task-specific golden sets per product with exact-match and LLM-as-judge checks, targeting under 5 minutes. Stage 2 runs on alias promotion candidates: the platform's cross-cutting shadow eval set (300-1,000 curated cases across all use-case categories), plus regression checks against the last 30 days of sampled live traffic scored offline — this catches distribution shift invisible to curated golden sets. Stage 3 is business-metric monitoring in production: conversion, user satisfaction, downstream pipeline error rates — LLM quality ultimately has to map to a business number, and the eval pipeline should predict it. The governance model is: Stage 1 failure blocks the PR; Stage 2 failure blocks alias promotion; Stage 3 degradation triggers a rollback. The eval harness itself is versioned and tested like application code — an eval bug that lets a regression through is a production incident.
fj: We run a quick eval before deploying to make sure it looks reasonable.
fs: Three-stage gate: CI golden sets (fast, PR-blocking) → shadow eval on alias promotion (cross-cutting, covers distribution) → business-metric monitoring in prod (rollback trigger) — each stage catches what the others miss.

followups.q: How do you prevent eval dataset contamination from leaking into model training?
followups.a: Never publish your golden sets externally. Store them in a private artifact store, redact them from logs, and rotate a portion quarterly with fresh production examples. If you're working with a provider to fine-tune on your data, contractually exclude eval sets from training data and verify via a held-out canary — a small synthetic set with known answers that a model trained on it would score perfectly but a clean model would score at baseline.

followups.q: How do you correlate Stage 1 eval scores with Stage 3 business metrics to validate the eval is measuring what matters?
followups.a: Run a retrospective analysis: for each past model or prompt change, compare the delta in Stage 1 score to the delta in the business metric (e.g. task completion rate, user satisfaction). Compute Spearman correlation. A correlation below 0.5 means your eval proxy is not predictive and you're optimizing a metric decoupled from the business outcome — update the eval criteria. This calibration loop is what separates a vanity eval from a decision-making gate.

q: How do you design LLM cost governance at the platform level — setting budgets, enforcing them in real time, and creating visibility that engineering teams actually act on?
a: Cost governance has three components: enforcement, visibility, and accountability. Enforcement: every LLM call routes through the gateway with a virtual key tagged to team-id and feature-name; each key carries a monthly budget cap and a daily soft limit; the gateway returns 429 BudgetExceeded automatically. Visibility: a Grafana dashboard fed from gateway logs showing spend per team per model per feature, 7-day and 30-day trends, and token efficiency (output quality / $ — not just spend). Accountability: monthly cost review where teams present their spend trend, explain spikes, and commit to efficiency improvements; the platform team publishes a quarterly efficiency leaderboard ranking teams by cost-per-successful-task. The governance failure mode is invisible spend — a team uses an expensive model for a task a cheaper model handles equally well, nobody notices, and the bill just grows. The leaderboard and dashboard make that visible without becoming a policing exercise.
fj: We look at the API billing page each month to see how much we spent.
fs: Enforcement via gateway virtual-key budget caps, visibility via per-team cost dashboards with token efficiency metrics, accountability via monthly spend reviews and a quarterly efficiency leaderboard — invisible spend is the failure mode.

followups.q: How do you handle a team that keeps hitting its budget cap because their use case genuinely requires expensive calls?
followups.a: Run a structured cost-efficiency review: what model are they using, what's the task, does a cheaper model clear the quality SLO? If yes, help them migrate. If no, document why and raise their budget cap with a business justification — not as a rubber stamp, but as an explicit cost-vs-value decision recorded in the team's planning documents. The goal is intentional spend, not minimum spend.

followups.q: What token efficiency metric do you use to compare teams fairly across different use cases?
followups.a: Cost per successful task completion — not total spend or tokens per call, which penalize high-volume or complex tasks. Define task success per feature (structured output parse success, downstream pipeline success, human-eval score above threshold) and divide monthly cost by successful task count. This normalizes for volume and complexity and focuses the conversation on whether the model spend is creating value, not just whether it is large.

q: How do you manage model reliability at scale — designing for provider outages, model-version drift, and silent quality degradation across 50+ LLM-powered features?
a: Reliability at scale requires three overlapping defenses. Availability: all features route through the gateway with automatic fallback configured — primary provider down triggers fallback to a secondary provider or a self-hosted open model, with cooldown to prevent retry storms. Quality drift detection: every feature logs a quality proxy (structured output parse success, faithfulness score from LLM-as-judge, downstream pipeline success rate) and emits it as a metric with feature-level alerting thresholds; a 5% drop in any metric opens a PagerDuty alert. The drift signal must be feature-specific — an aggregate quality metric hides per-feature degradations. Model version management: all provider model identifiers are resolved through the model registry so a provider's silent alias swap (e.g. 'gpt-4o' pointing to a new underlying version) is detectable in gateway logs, and the registry's alias promotion process requires re-running Stage 2 evals. The failure mode this prevents is a provider silently swapping the model behind an alias, degrading three features before anyone notices.
fj: If something goes down we'll notice from user complaints.
fs: Three defenses: gateway fallback for availability; per-feature quality-proxy metrics with alerting for drift detection; model registry that surfaces provider alias swaps before they hit production at scale.

followups.q: How do you set quality-proxy alert thresholds across 50 features without spending months calibrating each?
followups.a: Start with relative thresholds: alert when a 7-day rolling average drops more than 5% relative to the prior 7-day window for any feature. This auto-scales with baseline performance and catches sudden drops without requiring feature-by-feature absolute calibration. Refine to absolute thresholds for the highest-criticality features after enough data to establish a stable baseline. The relative threshold catches regressions; the absolute threshold prevents drift below an unacceptable floor.

followups.q: What's the difference between provider downtime and model quality drift, and how do you operationally separate them?
followups.a: Provider downtime shows up in gateway error rate (429s, 5xx, timeouts) within seconds — your availability alerting catches it. Quality drift shows up in your quality-proxy metrics over hours or days with no API errors — the model is responding but its behavior changed. Separate alerting paths: error rate from gateway logs for availability; quality scores from your async eval pipeline for drift. Conflating them means a slow quality regression looks like 'everything is fine' until it's severe.

q: How do you set a fine-tune-vs-RAG-vs-API strategy as a platform standard — what's the decision rubric, and how do you enforce it without becoming a bottleneck to 15 product teams?
a: Define the rubric as a three-question decision tree documented in the platform's engineering guide: (1) Is the gap primarily about knowledge or facts the model lacks? → RAG first. (2) Is the gap about consistent behavior, format, or domain style with stable labeled data? → Fine-tuning. (3) Does a strong system prompt + few-shot in the current model clear the SLO at acceptable cost? → Prompt engineering first; always try this before escalating. Enforce without becoming a bottleneck by making the decision self-serve: publish the decision tree, provide a starter eval harness teams run themselves, and require a one-page decision memo only for fine-tune or self-host requests (high commitment). The platform reviews memos in a weekly 30-minute slot — not as an approver but as a consultant offering alternatives and sharing prior decisions. The bottleneck trap is requiring platform sign-off on every prompt change; the right gate is the high-cost path, not the default path.
fj: We decide case by case — the team lead picks what they think is best.
fs: Document the three-question decision tree (knowledge gap → RAG; behavior gap → fine-tune; prompt first always), self-serve eval harness, one-page memo only for fine-tune/self-host — platform consults, doesn't block.

followups.q: How do you handle a team that fine-tuned a model for a task that prompt engineering would have solved?
followups.a: Don't relitigate the decision — help them measure whether the fine-tune is actually outperforming a well-prompted baseline (often it isn't). If it isn't, document the lesson in the platform's decision guide with a concrete example: 'We fine-tuned for X; retrospectively, a system prompt with three examples matched quality at zero ops cost.' That case study is worth more than a policy, because it gives teams data to self-correct next time rather than a rule they resent.

q: As the principal owner of AI strategy for an engineering org, how do you set a multi-year model portfolio — which capabilities to build internally, which to buy from vendors, and how do you avoid lock-in?
a: A multi-year AI portfolio decision starts with capability classification: frontier reasoning and language capabilities are commodity inputs you buy (the ROI of building your own LLM is negative for every org except a handful of labs), while your domain-specific data, eval sets, and fine-tuned adapters are the durable differentiators you own. Avoid lock-in through provider abstraction at the gateway layer and model aliases that decouple application code from provider identifiers; the rule is that a full provider migration must be possible within one sprint without application code changes. For the three-year roadmap: Year 1, standardize on a gateway and eval infrastructure — this is the foundation; Year 2, build the fine-tuning and adapter management capability for high-value vertical use cases where you have proprietary data and stable task definitions; Year 3, evaluate self-hosting for your two or three highest-volume commodity tasks where open models have crossed quality parity and cost crossover has been reached. Review annually because the model landscape shifts faster than a three-year plan can anticipate — the roadmap is a direction with explicit revision gates, not a committed plan.
fj: We commit to one provider and go deep — it's simpler.
fs: Buy frontier capability (commodity), own domain data + eval + fine-tuned adapters (durable moat), abstract providers at gateway to enable one-sprint migration, roadmap: infra → fine-tuning → selective self-host, with annual revision gates.

followups.q: How do you communicate the AI portfolio strategy to a board or executive leadership who want to know if you're 'using AI enough'?
followups.a: Frame it as a value-capture argument, not a technology choice: present the cost per successful AI-powered task trending down year-over-year, the number of product features shipping LLM capabilities, and the proportion of manual work automated. Boards understand unit economics and output metrics, not model names. Include a risk slide showing how provider abstraction and eval gates protect the company from vendor lock-in and silent regressions — those are real business risks framed as governance, not technical debt.

followups.q: How do you structure the governance body that owns the multi-year AI platform roadmap without it becoming bureaucratic?
followups.a: An AI platform council with rotating representatives from the three to five largest LLM-consuming teams plus the platform lead, meeting monthly for 45 minutes. Agenda: one metric review (cost, quality, reliability), one decision item (new capability request or vendor evaluation), and one lesson from a recent incident or retrospective. No decisions require more than one meeting — if a decision takes two meetings it's either not ready or not important enough. The council sets direction; individual teams execute without council approval on routine decisions.

followups.q: How do you prevent the 'AI strategy' from becoming an LLM strategy — missing non-LLM ML that still delivers more value for certain tasks?
followups.a: Require every AI initiative proposal to answer: 'Why an LLM and not a classical ML model or deterministic code?' Make the answer concrete — e.g. the task requires semantic language understanding over unstructured text, the domain changes too fast for a labeled training set, or no classical classifier cleared the quality SLO. This surfaces cases where a fine-tuned BERT or a simple classifier would deliver the same outcome at 1% of the LLM cost. Include classical ML as a first-class option in the decision rubric, not a footnote.

q: How do you design the safety and governance framework for LLM deployments at an org that handles regulated data — HIPAA, GDPR, and SOC2 — across multiple business units?
a: Safety and compliance for regulated LLM deployments requires layered controls at four levels. Data boundary: enforce that no regulated data leaves your network perimeter — this means self-hosted inference or a provider with signed BAA/DPA agreements and data-residency guarantees, and it is a hard constraint that overrides cost or capability arguments. Input/output controls: gateway-level PII detection and redaction before prompts leave the perimeter, output screening for sensitive information leakage, and audit logging of every request with PII scrubbed — retain logs for the compliance period. Access control: virtual keys scoped to minimum data classes, role-based access to the gateway admin, and quarterly access reviews. Incident response: a documented runbook for AI-specific incidents (prompt injection, data leakage, model misbehavior), with a severity classification that maps AI incidents to your existing incident management process. The governance body (your AI platform council or a dedicated AI risk committee) reviews the framework annually and after any significant provider change. The anti-pattern is treating LLM compliance as a one-time checkbox — model behavior and provider terms change, so governance is a living process.
fj: We add a disclaimer that the AI might make mistakes and that covers us.
fs: Four layers: data boundary (BAA/DPA + no regulated data outside perimeter), gateway PII redaction + audit logs, virtual-key access control with quarterly reviews, AI-specific incident runbook — governance is a living process, not a checkbox.

followups.q: How do you handle a provider that changes their data retention or training policy mid-contract?
followups.a: Detect it through automated monitoring of provider ToS change feeds and quarterly manual review by your legal and security team leads. When a policy changes, assess impact against your data classification matrix: does the change affect any data category you send to that provider? If yes, activate your provider migration runbook — swap the gateway alias to an alternative, validate with your eval harness, and complete migration before the new policy takes effect. Provider policy risk is why you maintain the abstraction layer and keep migration under one sprint.

followups.q: What does a useful AI-specific incident runbook contain that a general software incident runbook doesn't?
followups.a: AI-specific severity criteria: prompt injection is a P1 (active attack); silent quality regression is a P2 (user harm accumulating but no outage); hallucination that produced incorrect medical or financial advice is a P1 regardless of volume. AI-specific containment actions: disable the affected feature flag without rolling back the whole deployment, route to a cached response or a rule-based fallback, and increase sampling rate on the LLM-as-judge to surface the scope. Post-mortem template that includes: what changed in model or prompt that enabled the failure, what eval would have caught it, and what new test is being added. General runbooks don't distinguish these from API outages.

q: How do you set the org-wide standard for what goes into a system prompt versus what belongs in your application code, and why does this architectural boundary matter at scale?
a: The boundary is a safety and velocity boundary, not just a style question. System prompts are the model's behavioral specification — role, output format, constraint rules, and personas — and should be versioned, tested, and deployed through the same process as application code. What never goes in a system prompt: business logic that should fail loudly (put it in code where you get exceptions), authorization decisions (a model that 'knows' a user is an admin is not a security boundary — check it in your auth layer), and runtime data that changes per-request (inject it at the user turn or through retrieved context, not the system prompt, to keep the cached prefix stable). What always goes in the system prompt: the stable behavioral contract that applies to every user in the session. At scale, the failure mode is prompt sprawl — logic scattered between system prompts, user turns, and application code with no clear ownership, making any change risky and no one knowing which layer controls what. Enforce the boundary by treating system prompts as configuration artifacts owned by the feature team, reviewed in code review, stored in version control alongside the service, and covered by the team's eval golden set.
fj: We put everything in the system prompt — it's the easiest way to control the model.
fs: System prompt = behavioral contract (role/format/constraints), versioned and tested; business logic + auth in code (not prompt); runtime per-request data in user turn after cache breakpoint — prompt sprawl is the org-scale failure mode.

followups.q: How do you roll back a bad system prompt change in production across 15 teams' services?
followups.a: Each team's system prompt is version-controlled and deployed as a versioned config alongside the service. A bad prompt rollback is a config rollback — the same process as rolling back a feature flag, not a code deploy. At the platform level, the model registry's alias and the team's prompt version are independent axes: you can roll back the prompt without touching the model, and vice versa. If a team doesn't version their prompts in source control, they have no rollback — this is the primary enforcement lever for requiring prompt versioning.
